title: How to handle the workplace with a regulated nervous system
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The morning meeting is already going sideways

Your colleague interrupts you for the third time. Your chest tightens. Your throat closes. You feel that familiar surge of heat behind your eyes and you think, not here, not now, please not now. And then you smile and nod and say something agreeable while your nervous system screams that you are not safe.

I know this scene. I've lived it hundreds of times. Maybe thousands.

Here's the thing. The workplace was not designed for nervous systems that have been shaped by trauma. Open floor plans, sudden feedback sessions, performance reviews, that Slack notification sound that makes your stomach drop. These are not neutral environments. They are trigger machines disguised as professional settings.

And the advice you usually get? Breathe. Meditate. Set boundaries. Good luck with that when your boss is standing over your desk and your body has already decided that compliance equals survival.

So let's talk about what actually works. Not the wellness industry's version of regulation. The real version. The one that acknowledges your nervous system has been trained for decades to detect threat and respond before you even know what's happening.

Because you can't think your way out of a dysregulated state. But you can learn to work with your body instead of against it.

What regulation actually looks like at work

Regulation doesn't mean you're calm all the time. Let me say that again because it matters. Regulation doesn't mean you feel peaceful or centered or any of those words that make me want to throw something. Regulation means your nervous system can move between states without getting stuck. You can feel frustration without losing your mind. You can feel anxiety without dissociating. You can feel anger without exploding or collapsing.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. This happens below conscious awareness. Your body decides whether you're safe before your brain has a chance to weigh in. That's why you can't just tell yourself to calm down and expect it to work. Your nervous system doesn't speak English. It speaks sensation, posture, facial expression, tone of voice.

So when you're in a meeting and someone criticizes your work, your body might go into a dorsal vagal shutdown. You go blank. You can't find words. You feel like you're underwater. Or you might go into a sympathetic surge. Your heart races. Your muscles tense. You want to fight or flee but you can't do either so you just sit there vibrating with adrenaline.

Both of these are normal. Both of these are survival responses. Neither of them means something is wrong with you.

But here's what I've learned after years of navigating corporate environments with a trauma history. You can build capacity. You can expand your window of tolerance. You can learn to notice the early signals of dysregulation and intervene before your system goes into full emergency mode.

It's not about never getting triggered. It's about what happens after.

The micro-practices that actually help

I'm not going to tell you to do a breathing exercise in the middle of a tense meeting. That's not realistic for most of us and frankly it can feel humiliating. But there are things you can do that are invisible to everyone else and genuinely effective.

One of the most powerful tools I've found comes from Peter Levine's work on somatic experiencing. It's called orienting. You simply look around the room and notice things you can see. The color of the wall. The shape of a lamp. The pattern on someone's shirt. This might sound too simple to matter but it's actually doing something . It's telling your nervous system that you are here in this room, not back in whatever past situation your brain has activated. You are orienting to the present moment through your senses.

Another practice that helps is finding your edge. This is something Pat Ogden talks about in sensorimotor psychotherapy. You notice where in your body you feel the activation. Maybe it's tightness in your chest. Maybe it's a knot in your stomach. Maybe it's that hollow feeling in your throat. You just notice it without trying to change it. You stay with the sensation for a few seconds. And then you gently move your attention to something neutral in the room. Back and forth. Sensation, neutral. Sensation, neutral. This builds your capacity to tolerate discomfort without getting pulled under.

I also use something Deb Dana calls glimmers. These are small moments of safety and connection that your nervous system can latch onto. The warmth of your coffee cup. The way light comes through the window. A colleague's small smile. I deliberately collect these throughout the day. They're anchors. They remind my system that not everything is a threat.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic has helped me understand why certain workplace interactions feel so devastating. He explains that when you have complex trauma, your nervous system can flash back to childhood dynamics of powerlessness without any conscious memory. That feeling of being small and helpless in a meeting? That's an emotional flashback. And knowing that changes everything.

Stay with me here because this next part is crucial. The goal is not to never feel triggered. The goal is to recognize when you're in a flashback and bring yourself back to the present. Walker calls this the 13 Steps for Managing Flashbacks. I've memorized them. I use them in bathroom stalls and empty conference rooms and sometimes just sitting at my desk with my eyes open staring at a spreadsheet.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz developed Internal Family Systems, and his framework has been invaluable for me at work. When I notice a part of me that wants to people-please or a part that wants to shut down completely, I can get curious instead of judgmental. Oh, there's the protector who keeps me safe by making me invisible. I see you. Thank you for doing your job. I've got it from here.

That internal conversation takes maybe 15 seconds. Nobody knows it's happening. But it changes everything.

The problem with workplace wellness culture

I need to be honest with you about something. The wellness industry has done real damage to trauma survivors in the workplace. We've been told to meditate our way out of nervous system dysregulation. We've been told to practice gratitude when we're in a freeze state. We've been told to breathe deeply when what we actually need is to feel safe enough to exist in our own skin.

Toxic positivity is not regulation. It's suppression dressed up as healing.

When your boss tells you to just take a deep breath, what your nervous system hears is: your response is unacceptable. You need to perform calmness so everyone else can feel comfortable. Your survival reaction is inconvenient for the team.

That's not support. That's control.

Real regulation at work requires something that most workplaces are not willing to offer. It requires the ability to actually leave a situation when you're overwhelmed. It requires the freedom to say, I need a minute. It requires not being penalized for having a body that responds to threat.

I'm not saying you can single-handedly change your workplace culture. But you can stop gaslighting yourself about what you need. You can stop believing that something is wrong with you because you can't calm down on command. Your nervous system is not broken. It's doing exactly what it was trained to do.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you concrete exercises for tracking triggers, identifying flashbacks, and building self-compassion. I've used it to map out which workplace situations activate which parts of me. That knowledge is power. It's the difference between being blindsided by your own reactions and being able to say, ah yes, this is the pattern, I know this one.

What recovery actually asks of you

Here's the uncomfortable truth. You might need to change jobs. You might need to leave a field entirely. You might need to work for yourself or work part-time or work in a completely different way than you imagined.

I know that's not what you want to hear. I know you want to be able to function in any environment. I wanted that too. But the reality is that some workplaces are genuinely toxic. They are not safe for any nervous system, let alone one that has been shaped by trauma. And staying in those environments will keep you stuck in survival mode no matter how many skills you have.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, showed us that trauma lives in the body. You cannot think your way out of it. You cannot meditate your way out of it. You cannot work hard enough to earn safety. Safety is a birthright, not a reward for productivity.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work on self-compassion has helped me stop fighting my own nervous system. When I notice myself spiraling after a difficult interaction, I can say, this is hard. This is really hard. And instead of trying to fix it, I can just let it be hard for a moment. That acceptance is more regulating than any technique I've ever tried.

So here's what I want you to take from this. Your nervous system is not your enemy. It's trying to protect you. It's doing its job. The problem is that it learned to do that job in a context that no longer applies. And unlearning those patterns takes time. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to be uncomfortable without abandoning yourself.

You can handle the workplace with a regulated nervous system. But regulation is not a destination you arrive at. It's a practice. It's a return. Over and over again. You get dysregulated and you come back. You get triggered and you orient. You get flooded and you find your edge.

And maybe, eventually, you build a life where you don't have to be so vigilant all the time. Maybe you find work that doesn't require you to override your instincts. Maybe you find people who can hold your dysregulation without needing you to fix it.

But right now, in this moment, you just need to know one thing. You are not broken. Your nervous system is not broken. And the fact that you're still here, still trying, still showing up despite everything your body has been through. That's not weakness. That's the most strength I can imagine.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding emotional flashbacks and the inner critic
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems and parts work
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook with exercises for tracking triggers and building self-compassion
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
